subject: Mathematics
solution:
Plotting the truth table of the following option we have,

p q ~p ~q ~p∨q
T T F F T
T F F T F
F T T F T
F F T T T

We know that, A⇒B is only false when A is true and B is false, now using this rule and above table we will make truth table of options,

~p∨q⇒p p⇒~p∨q ~p∨q⇒q q⇒~p∨q
T T T T
T F T T
F T T T
F T F T

 

Hence q⇒~p∨q is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

((A∧(B∨C))⇒(A∨B))⇒A

We know that, X⇒Y≡~X∨Y,

Now using the above formula we get,

≡~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B)⇒A

≡~(~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B))∨A

≡(A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)∨A

Now to solve (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B) let us assume a universal set 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8, set A=1,2,3, set B=3,4,5 and set C=5,6,7

So, A∧B∨C will be 3 and ~(A∨B) will be 6,7,8, 

So, (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)=∅

≡F∨A

≡A

∴ Negation of statement=~A

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Logical Statement p→q is false if and only if p is true and q is false.

So, when p is false, q is true, p∨q→p∨~q will be false.

Clearly p∨q→p∨~q is not a tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
~~p∨q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

=p∧~q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

By distributive law

=p∧~q∨r∧~q∧r

p∧~q∧r≡p∧r∧~q

=p∧∼q∧r

By Associative law

=p∧r∧~q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We have,

S1: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r

For S1:  If p is F, q is T, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r≡T

So, p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r≡F

then F⇒F≡F

i.e. S1 is not a tautology

S2: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r

S2:  If p is T, q is F, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r∨q⇒r≡T

then p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r≡F

i.e. S2 is not a tautology

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given condition is, p∧q→r∨q∧p∧r→q).....(1)

We know conditional disjunction as,

p→q≡~p∨q

Hence, p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q

Using De Morgan's law

p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q≡~p∨~r∨q

Equation 1 can be written as,

⇒p∧q→r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∧q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒(~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨t∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~r∨q

For above statement to be a tautology,

r can be ~p or q

When r=~p or r=q⇒~p∨~r∨q=t

∴ Two value of r are possible

subject: Mathematics
solution:
1 (p⇒q)∨(~q⇒p)

By applying a⇒b≡~a∨b & ~~a=a, we get

=(~p∨q)∨(q∨p)

Since, the operation disjunction is commutative, hence, we have

=(~p∨q)∨(p∨q)

Now, applying the distributive law i.e. a∨b∨c≡a∨b∨a∨c, we get

=(~p∨p)∨q

Also, we know that ~a∨a≡t & t∨a≡t, where t represents tautology.

Thus, we have

=t∨q=t.

Hence, the option A is a tautology.

2 (q⇒p)∨(~q⇒p)

Again, using the same definitions, we get

=(~q∨p)∨(q∨p)

=(~q∨q)∨p

=t∨p=t.

Hence, the option B is also a tautology.

3 (p⇒~q)∨(~q⇒p)

=(~p∨~q)∨(q∨p)

=(~p∨p)∨(~q∨q)

=t∨t=t.

Hence, the option C is also a tautology.

4 (~p⇒q)∨(~q⇒p)

=(p∨q)∨(q∨p)

=(p∨q)∨(p∨q)

=p∨q.

Which is not a tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Negation of x↔~y

≡~x↔~y

≡~x→~y∧~y→x

≡~x→~y∨~~y→x (using De'Morgan's laws)

≡x∧y∨~y∧~x

subject: Mathematics
solution:
LHS of all the options are same i.e. ((P→Q)∧~Q)

≡(~P∨Q)∧~Q

≡(~P∧~Q)∨(Q∧~Q)

≡~P∧~Q

(A) (~P∧~Q)→Q

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨Q

≡(P∨Q)∨Q≠ tautology

(B) (~P∧~Q)→~P

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨~P

≡(P∨Q)∨~P

(C) (~P∧~Q)→P

≡(P∨Q)∨P≠ Tautology

(D) (~P∧~Q)→(P∧Q)

≡(P∨Q)∨(P∧Q)≠ Tautology

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

p∨q∧q∨~r

Now negation of above expression will be,

~p∨q∧q∨~r

=~p∨q∨~q∨~r as ~A∧B≡~A∨~B

=~p∧~q∨~q∧r

=~p∧~q∨~q∧~p∧~q∨r

=~q∧~p∨r∧~q∨r

=~q∧~p∨r∧~q∧~q∨r

=~q∧~p∨r∧~q

=~p∨r∧~q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using logical equivalence condition p→q≡~p∨q, given statement can be expressed as, 

p∧~q→p→~q

≡ ~p∧~q∨~p∨~q.......(1)

We know De Morgan's law of logical equivalence is,

~p∧q≡~p∨~q

Applying De Morgan's law in equation(1) we get,

≡ ~p∨q∨~p∨~q   ∵~~p≡p

≡ ~p∨~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∨t≡t (tautology)

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that A→B is false, when A is true and B is false

So, for P∧~R→~R∧Q to be false, P∧~R should be true and ~R∧Q is false.

i.e P is true, R is false and Q is false.

A B A→B
T T T
T F F
F T T
F F T

Now, 

for P∨Q→~R is F→T≡T

R∨Q→~P is F→F≡T

~P∨Q→~R is T→T≡T

~R∨Q→~P is T→F≡F

subject: Mathematics
options: if I will go to the doctor, then I am not feeling well.
if I am feeling well, then I will not go to the doctor.
if I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
if I will go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
solution:
Statement: If I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor.

Let p: I am not feeling well and q: I will go to the doctor.

The given statement is p→q.

We know that the contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

Contrapositive: If I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
